Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside, Open And Or Vacate Judgment Of Dismissal Of Action With Prejudice, After Sustaining Of Demurrer To Complaint Without Leave To Amend And Order Granting Defendants Demurrer To Complaint, If Entered
Off Calendar Dec-23-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Elina Protich, Esq. of FIDUCIARY LAW ATTORNEYS, T:415.759.8755 for defendants/opposing party, with defendants Phyllis Gardner (on Court Call) and Gordon Priatko;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF JOHN PRIATKO's Motion To Set Aside, Open And Or Vacate Judgment Of Dismissal Of Action With Prejudice, After Sustaining Of Demurrer To Complaint Without Leave To Amend And Order Granting Defendants Demurrer To Complaint, If Entered, as follows:

Plaintiff John Priatko's motion to set aside, open, and/or vacate judgment of dismissal of action with prejudice is DENIED. Plaintiff was properly served and had actual notice of the August 28, 2017 hearing on Defendants' demurrer, which ultimately was sustained without leave to amend. Code of Civil Procedure 473.5, therefore, does not provide grounds to set aside the judgment. Nor is the judgment void or contain clerical mistakes to be corrected. (Code Civ. Proc., 473(d).)

Plaintiff has not made a showing of "mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect" sufficient for the Court to grant relief under Code of Civil Procedure 473(b). Plaintiff argues he was in the hospital the morning of the August 28, 2017 hearing. However, Plaintiff's motion reveals he was able to file an untimely opposition to the demurrer the morning of the hearing and his hospital intake form shows he was not admitted until 8:17 p.m. (Plaintiff's Motion, Ex. D.)

Furthermore, Plaintiff waited over two years after the entry of the order sustaining Defendants' demurrer without leave to amend and six months after the dismissal of the action to file this motion. "In order to qualify for relief under section 473, the moving party must act diligently in seeking relief." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 234.) If there is a substantial delay between discovery of the default and defendant's filing a motion for relief under 473(b), defendant must show a reasonable excuse for the delay. (Ludka v. Memory Magnetics Int'l (1972) 25 Cal.App.3d 316, 321.) Delays of three months or more routinely result in denial of relief absent satisfactory explanation for the delay. (See Stafford v. Mach (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1184; see also Minick v. City of Petaluma (2016) 3 Cal.App.5th 15, 34 ["A delay is unreasonable as a matter of law only when it exceeds three months and there is no evidence to explain the delay."].) Plaintiff offers none.

After the six-month period for statutory relief has passed, the court may still grant relief on equitable grounds, including extrinsic fraud or mistake. (Manson, Iver & York v. Black (2009) 176 Cal.App.4th 36, 47.) There is no evidence of extrinsic fraud or mistake to indicate Plaintiff was prevented from filing a timely opposition to the demurrer or from appearing in Court for the hearing on demurrer. To the contrary, the record shows that the Court was very flexible and granted a continuance when Plaintiff requested moving the hearing from July 26, 2017 to August 28, 2017. Plaintiff filed a late opposition to the demurrer on the morning of the August 2017 hearing, which included the date in which he was to appear. Plaintiff contends that opposing counsel and the Court were notified prior to the hearing of the demurrer that he could not participate. Plaintiff argues the hearing should have been continued, but Plaintiff does not attach copies of the correspondence he refers to or any requests that the hearing be continued a second time.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
